moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Ware Opening, a rare sideline where we immediately advance our rook's pawn. While it looks offbeat, this move has a specific purpose: we're preparing to support the a-file and potentially fianchetto our light-squared bishop later. More importantly, we're keeping Black guessing and avoiding the theoretical battles of 1.e4 or 1.d4—this is the opening's main appeal at club level. The move is unconventional but playable if you have a clear plan afterward.

Black takes the center immediately, claiming space in the middle and preparing to develop pieces actively from f6 and c6. This is a natural response to our passive start, and Black rightly decides not to wait around while we execute an unclear plan. With e5, Black has seized the initiative and established a strong presence on e5 and d4, making our rook on a3 look even more out of play for now.

Now we execute the trap's key idea: we're preparing Ra3-b3 or Ra3-c3 to pressure the b7 or c7 squares, but more importantly, if Black plays d5 or d6 in the future, we've positioned our rook to potentially harass Black's pieces or control critical lines. This move is highly unusual and depends on Black making inaccurate moves—against solid play, Black simply develops normally and we've wasted tempi. The 'Meadow Hay Trap' only works if Black cooperates, so be ready to pivot to normal chess if they don't fall into the trap.
